prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is the original verbatim passage? The other passages are paraphrased versions of it.

Options:
A. In the July 2014 general election for the Cook Islands constituency of Mitiaro, a by-election was held. The election ended in a tie with incumbent Democratic Party MP Tangata Vavia receiving 50 votes, while Cook Islands Party challenger Tuakeu Tangatapoto also received 50 votes. The High Court called a by-election, but the count was postponed due to a legal challenge by the Cook Islands Party. The court found that one person had voted illegally in the Mitiaro constituency, invalidated that person.
B. A by-election was held in the Cook Islands constituency of Mitiaro on 11 November 2014.In the July 2014 general election, the contest for the Mitiaro seat resulted in a tie. Incumbent MP Tangata Vavia, of the Democratic Party, received 50 votes, but so did Cook Islands Party challenger Tuakeu Tangatapoto. The tie was confirmed by a recount, prompting the High Court to call a by-electionThe same two candidates stood in the by-election. The by-election took place, but counting was delayed due to a legal challenge by the Cook Islands Party. The court ultimately ruled that one person had voted illegally in the Mitiaro constituency in the general election, and invalidated that person's ballot. This resulted in Democratic Party candidate Tangata Vavia being declared elected by 50 votes to
C. On November 11, 2014, a special election, known as a by-election, occurred in the Mitiaro electoral district of the Cook Islands. The initial general election in July 2014 for the Mitiaro parliamentary seat concluded with an equal number of votes for the two leading candidates. Tangata Vavia, the sitting Member of Parliament representing the Democratic Party, secured 50 votes, a total matched precisely by Tuakeu Tangatapoto, his opponent from the Cook Islands Party. A subsequent recount verified this deadlock, which then led the High Court to mandate a new election.

The same two individuals who contested the general election also competed in the subsequent by-election. Although the by-election proceeded as scheduled, the tabulation of votes was postponed due to a legal objection filed by the Cook Islands Party. Following judicial review, the court determined that one individual had cast an unlawful ballot within the Mitiaro constituency during the general election, and consequently, that particular vote was nullified. As a direct consequence of this ruling, Tangata Vavia, the candidate from the Democratic Party, was formally recognized as the victor with 50 votes to.

Answer:

options.A: In the July 2014 general election for the Cook Islands constituency of Mitiaro, a by-election was held. The election ended in a tie with incumbent Democratic Party MP Tangata Vavia receiving 50 votes, while Cook Islands Party challenger Tuakeu Tangatapoto also received 50 votes. The High Court called a by-election, but the count was postponed due to a legal challenge by the Cook Islands Party. The court found that one person had voted illegally in the Mitiaro constituency, invalidated that person.
options.B: A by-election was held in the Cook Islands constituency of Mitiaro on 11 November 2014.In the July 2014 general election, the contest for the Mitiaro seat resulted in a tie. Incumbent MP Tangata Vavia, of the Democratic Party, received 50 votes, but so did Cook Islands Party challenger Tuakeu Tangatapoto. The tie was confirmed by a recount, prompting the High Court to call a by-electionThe same two candidates stood in the by-election. options.C:
On November 11, 2014, a special election, known as a by-election, occurred in the Mitiaro electoral district of the Cook Islands. The initial general election in July 2014 for the Mitiaro parliamentary seat concluded with an equal number of votes for the two leading candidates. Tangata Vavia, the sitting Member of Parliament representing the Democratic Party, secured 50 votes, a total matched precisely by Tuakeu Tangatapoto, his opponent from the Cook Islands Party. A subsequent recount verified this deadlock, which then led the High Court to mandate a new election.

The same two individuals who contested the general election also competed in the subsequent by-election. Although the by-election proceeded as scheduled, the tabulation of votes was postponed due to a legal objection filed by the Cook Islands Party. Following judicial review, the court determined that one individual had cast an unlawful ballot within the Mitiaro constituency during the general election, and consequently, that particular vote was nullified. As a direct consequence of this ruling, Tangata Vavia, the candidate from the Democratic Party, was formally recognized as the victor with 50 votes to.
